import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFinset"
Level 10

Title "Image, Union, and Set Difference"

Introduction "
# Union + Image + Sdiff Integration

Prove that removing the image of $s$ from the image of $s \\cup t$
leaves only elements from the image of $t$:

$$f(s \\cup t) \\setminus f(s) \\;\\subseteq\\; f(t)$$

This integrates moves from FinsetOperations and FinsetImage:
subset, sdiff, union, image backward/forward, cases, and by_contra.

The interesting case: when the witness came from $s$, you can
build $y \\in f(s)$, which contradicts $y \\notin f(s)$.
"

/-- Elements in the image of a union but not in the image of s must come from t. -/
Statement (f : ÔäĽ Ôćĺ ÔäĽ) (s t : Finset ÔäĽ) :
    (s Ôł¬ t).image f \ s.image f ÔŐć t.image f := by
  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
  intro y hy
  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at hy
  obtain ÔčĘhut, hnsÔčę := hy
  Hint "Extract the witness from `hut` using backward image membership,
  then determine whether the witness came from `s` or `t`."
  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hut
  obtain ÔčĘx, hx, hfxÔčę := hut
  rw [Finset.mem_union] at hx
  Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `hx` with `cases hx with`.
  In the `inl` case (`x Ôłł s`): use `by_contra` then `apply hns`
  to show `y Ôłł s.image f`, contradicting `hns`.
  In the `inr` case (`x Ôłł t`): build forward membership directly."
  cases hx with
  | inl hxs =>
    by_contra _
    apply hns
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    exact ÔčĘx, hxs, hfxÔčę
  | inr hxt =>
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    exact ÔčĘx, hxt, hfxÔčę

Conclusion "
This proof integrated 6 moves from two worlds:

| Move | Source |
|---|---|
| `rw [Finset.subset_iff]; intro y hy` | FinsetOperations |
| `rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at hy` | FinsetOperations |
| `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hut; obtain ...` | FinsetImage |
| `rw [Finset.mem_union] at hx; cases ...` | FinsetOperations |
| `by_contra; apply hns` | FinsetOperations |
| `rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ÔčĘx, ...Ôčę` | FinsetImage |

The `inl` case used `by_contra` to expose the contradiction:
if $x \\in s$, then $y = f(x) \\in f(s)$, contradicting $y \\notin f(s)$.
"

1Ôćĺimport Game.Metadata
     2Ôćĺ
     3ÔćĺWorld "PsetFinset"
     4ÔćĺLevel 11
     5Ôćĺ
     6ÔćĺTitle "Boss: Image Preserves Set Difference"
     7Ôćĺ
     8ÔćĺIntroduction "
     9Ôćĺ# Boss: Image and Set Difference Under Injectivity
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺGiven an **injective** function $f$, prove:
    12Ôćĺ
    13Ôćĺ$$(s \\setminus t).\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\subseteq\\; s.\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\setminus\\; t.\\text{image}\\; f$$
    14Ôćĺ
    15ÔćĺIf you remove elements of $t$ from $s$ *before* applying $f$,
    16Ôćĺthe result is contained in what you'd get by applying $f$ to $s$
    17Ôćĺand then removing the image of $t$.
    18Ôćĺ
    19ÔćĺThis proof integrates 7+ moves from FinsetOperations and FinsetImage.
    20ÔćĺNo new moves ÔÇö only moves you've already used.
    21Ôćĺ"
    22Ôćĺ
    23Ôćĺ/-- Under injectivity, image preserves set difference (as a subset). -/
    24ÔćĺStatement (f : ÔäĽ Ôćĺ ÔäĽ) (hf : Function.Injective f) (s t : Finset ÔäĽ) :
    25Ôćĺ    (s \ t).image f ÔŐć s.image f \ t.image f := by
    26Ôćĺ  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
    27Ôćĺ  intro y hy
    28Ôćĺ  Hint "Extract the witness from `hy`, unfold the source sdiff,
    29Ôćĺ  then build both parts of the target sdiff."
    30Ôćĺ  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy
    31Ôćĺ  obtain ÔčĘa, ha, hfaÔčę := hy
    32Ôćĺ  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at ha
    33Ôćĺ  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]
    34Ôćĺ  constructor
    35Ôćĺ  -- Part 1: y Ôłł s.image f
    36Ôćĺ  ┬Ě Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ÔčĘa, ha.1, hfaÔčę`"
    37Ôćĺ    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    38Ôćĺ    exact ÔčĘa, ha.1, hfaÔčę
    39Ôćĺ  -- Part 2: y Ôłë t.image f
    40Ôćĺ  ┬Ě Hint "The goal is `y Ôłë t.image f`. Assume `y Ôłł t.image f`,
    41Ôćĺ    extract a witness `b Ôłł t`, and use injectivity to derive `a = b`."
    42Ôćĺ    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro hyt`, then
    43Ôćĺ    `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt; obtain ÔčĘb, hbt, hfbÔčę := hyt`."
    44Ôćĺ    intro hyt
    45Ôćĺ    rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt
    46Ôćĺ    obtain ÔčĘb, hbt, hfbÔčę := hyt
    47Ôćĺ    Hint "You need `f a = f b` to apply injectivity. You have
    48Ôćĺ    `hfa : f a = y` and `hfb : f b = y` ÔÇö both sides equal `y`.
    49Ôćĺ    Use `rw` with both hypotheses to build the equality."
    50Ôćĺ    Hint (hidden := true) "`have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]`.
    51Ôćĺ    Then `have hab : a = b := hf hfab`.
    52Ôćĺ    `rw [hab] at ha; exact ha.2 hbt`."
    53Ôćĺ    have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]
    54Ôćĺ    have hab : a = b := hf hfab
    55Ôćĺ    rw [hab] at ha
    56Ôćĺ    exact ha.2 hbt
    57Ôćĺ
    58ÔćĺConclusion "
    59ÔćĺCongratulations ÔÇö you've completed the **Finset Problem Set**!
    60Ôćĺ
    61ÔćĺYou proved that injective functions preserve set difference:
    62Ôćĺ$f(s \\setminus t) \\subseteq f(s) \\setminus f(t)$ when $f$ is injective.
    63Ôćĺ
    64ÔćĺThe key insight: injectivity was essential. Without it, two
    65Ôćĺdifferent elements $a \\notin t$ and $b \\in t$ could map to
    66Ôćĺthe same $y$, making $y$ appear in $t.\\text{image}\\; f$ even though
    67Ôćĺit came from an element outside $t$. Injectivity forces $a = b$,
    68Ôćĺwhich creates the contradiction.
    69Ôćĺ
    70Ôćĺ**What's next**: The next world introduces **cardinality** ÔÇö
    71Ôćĺthe number of elements in a finset ÔÇö and the fundamental
    72Ôćĺcounting identities.
    73Ôćĺ"
    74Ôćĺ
    75Ôćĺ/-- `Finset.image_sdiff s t hf` states that when `f` is injective,
    76Ôćĺ`(s \\ t).image f = s.image f \\ t.image f` ÔÇö image distributes
    77Ôćĺover set difference under injectivity. -/
    78ÔćĺTheoremDoc Finset.image_sdiff as "Finset.image_sdiff" in "Finset"
    79Ôćĺ
    80Ôćĺ/-- `Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn` is the `InjOn` variant of
    81Ôćĺ`Finset.image_sdiff` ÔÇö requires injectivity only on the relevant sets. -/
    82ÔćĺTheoremDoc Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn as "Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn" in "Finset"
    83Ôćĺ
    84ÔćĺDisabledTactic trivial ┬źdecide┬╗ native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto push_neg linarith
    85ÔćĺDisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_image_of_mem Finset.image_subset_image Finset.image_mono Finset.image_union Finset.image_subset_iff Finset.subset_union_left Finset.subset_union_right Finset.mem_union_left Finset.mem_union_right le_sup_left le_sup_right Finset.image_inter Finset.image_inter_of_injOn Finset.image_inter_subset sdiff_sdiff_right_self Finset.image_sdiff Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn
    86Ôćĺ

Title "Boss: Image Preserves Set Difference"

Introduction "
# Boss: Image and Set Difference Under Injectivity

Given an **injective** function $f$, prove:

$$(s \\setminus t).\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\subseteq\\; s.\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\setminus\\; t.\\text{image}\\; f$$

If you remove elements of $t$ from $s$ *before* applying $f$,
the result is contained in what you'd get by applying $f$ to $s$
and then removing the image of $t$.

This proof integrates 7+ moves from FinsetOperations and FinsetImage.
No new moves ÔÇö only moves you've already used.
"

/-- Under injectivity, image preserves set difference (as a subset). -/
Statement (f : ÔäĽ Ôćĺ ÔäĽ) (hf : Function.Injective f) (s t : Finset ÔäĽ) :
    (s \ t).image f ÔŐć s.image f \ t.image f := by
  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
  intro y hy
  Hint "Extract the witness from `hy`, unfold the source sdiff,
  then build both parts of the target sdiff."
  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy
  obtain ÔčĘa, ha, hfaÔčę := hy
  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at ha
  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]
  constructor
  -- Part 1: y Ôłł s.image f
  ┬Ě Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Finset.mem_image]; exact ÔčĘa, ha.1, hfaÔčę`"
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    exact ÔčĘa, ha.1, hfaÔčę
  -- Part 2: y Ôłë t.image f
  ┬Ě Hint "The goal is `y Ôłë t.image f`. Assume `y Ôłł t.image f`,
    extract a witness `b Ôłł t`, and use injectivity to derive `a = b`."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro hyt`, then
    `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt; obtain ÔčĘb, hbt, hfbÔčę := hyt`."
    intro hyt
    rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt
    obtain ÔčĘb, hbt, hfbÔčę := hyt
    Hint "You need `f a = f b` to apply injectivity. You have
    `hfa : f a = y` and `hfb : f b = y` ÔÇö both sides equal `y`.
    Use `rw` with both hypotheses to build the equality."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]`.
    Then `have hab : a = b := hf hfab`.
    `rw [hab] at ha; exact ha.2 hbt`."
    have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]
    have hab : a = b := hf hfab
    rw [hab] at ha
    exact ha.2 hbt

Conclusion "
Congratulations ÔÇö you've completed the **Finset Problem Set**!

You proved that injective functions preserve set difference:
$f(s \\setminus t) \\subseteq f(s) \\setminus f(t)$ when $f$ is injective.

The key insight: injectivity was essential. Without it, two
different elements $a \\notin t$ and $b \\in t$ could map to
the same $y$, making $y$ appear in $t.\\text{image}\\; f$ even though
it came from an element outside $t$. Injectivity forces $a = b$,
which creates the contradiction.

**What's next**: The next world introduces **cardinality** ÔÇö
the number of elements in a finset ÔÇö and the fundamental
counting identities.
"

/-- `Finset.image_sdiff s t hf` states that when `f` is injective,
`(s \\ t).image f = s.image f \\ t.image f` ÔÇö image distributes
over set difference under injectivity. -/
TheoremDoc Finset.image_sdiff as "Finset.image_sdiff" in "Finset"

/-- `Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn` is the `InjOn` variant of
`Finset.image_sdiff` ÔÇö requires injectivity only on the relevant sets. -/
TheoremDoc Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn as "Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn" in "Finset"

file.content:
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 4

Title "Filter by Predicate"

Introduction "
# Filter: Selecting Elements by a Property

`s.filter p` keeps only the elements of `s` that satisfy predicate `p`.
The membership characterization is:

$$a \\in s.\\text{filter } p \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; a \\in s \\;\\land\\; p(a)$$

In Lean, this is `Finset.mem_filter`:
```
Finset.mem_filter : a Ôłł s.filter p Ôćö a Ôłł s Ôłž p a
```

The proof move: `rw [Finset.mem_filter]` converts a filter membership
goal into a conjunction. The first part is membership in the original
set, and the second part is that the predicate holds.

**Your task**: Prove that $4 \\in \\text{range}(6).\\text{filter}(n \\mapsto n \\% 2 = 0)$.

In words: 4 is in the set of even numbers less than 6.
"

/-- 4 is in the set of even numbers in range 6. -/
Statement : (4 : ÔäĽ) Ôłł (Finset.range 6).filter (fun n => n % 2 = 0) := by
  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_filter]` to split the filter membership
  into two parts: membership in the range, and the predicate."
  rw [Finset.mem_filter]
  Hint "The goal is `4 Ôłł Finset.range 6 Ôłž 4 % 2 = 0`. Use
  `constructor` to split it."
  constructor
  ┬Ě Hint "Prove `4 Ôłł Finset.range 6`. Recall from Finset Basics:
    `rw [Finset.mem_range]` converts this to `4 < 6`, then `omega`."
    rw [Finset.mem_range]
    omega
  ┬Ě Hint "Prove `4 % 2 = 0`. This is a concrete computation ÔÇö
    `rfl` handles it since `4 % 2` computes to `0`."
    rfl

Conclusion "
The pattern for filter membership:
1. `rw [Finset.mem_filter]` ÔÇö converts `a Ôłł s.filter p` to `a Ôłł s Ôłž p a`
2. `constructor` ÔÇö split the conjunction
3. Prove membership in `s` (the container)
4. Prove that `p a` holds (the predicate)

Notice the similarity to `mem_sdiff`: both produce conjunctions. The
difference is in the second component:
- **sdiff**: `a Ôłë t` (non-membership)
- **filter**: `p a` (predicate holds)

For concrete predicates like `n % 2 = 0`, the predicate goal often
reduces by computation and `rfl` closes it. For more complex predicates,
you may need `omega` or other tactics.

In plain mathematics: $x \\in \\{a \\in S \\mid P(a)\\}$ means $x \\in S$
and $P(x)$.

**Connection to set difference**: Filtering by a negated predicate
is closely related to set difference. If you think of `s.filter (fun x => x Ôłë t)`
as 'elements of `s` not in `t`,' that's exactly `s \\\\ t`. This
connection will deepen when you study complement reasoning later.

**Connection to intersection**: Filter with a membership predicate
*is* intersection: `s.filter (fun x => x Ôłł t) = s Ôłę t`. This follows
directly from `mem_filter` and `mem_inter` ÔÇö both say
'in `s` AND satisfies a condition.' Intersection is just the special
case where the condition is membership in another set.
"

/-- `Finset.mem_filter` states that `a Ôłł s.filter p Ôćö a Ôłł s Ôłž p a`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Finset.mem_filter]       -- in the goal
rw [Finset.mem_filter] at h  -- in a hypothesis
```

## When to use it
When the goal or a hypothesis involves `x Ôłł s.filter p`. After
rewriting, the goal becomes a conjunction: `x Ôłł s` and `p x`.

## Example
```
-- Goal: 4 Ôłł (Finset.range 6).filter (fun n => n % 2 = 0)
rw [Finset.mem_filter]
-- Goal: 4 Ôłł Finset.range 6 Ôłž 4 % 2 = 0
```
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.mem_filter as "Finset.mem_filter" in "Finset"

/-- `Finset.filter p s` keeps only the elements of `s` that satisfy
predicate `p`.

## Syntax
```
s.filter p           -- dot notation
Finset.filter p s    -- explicit
```

## Key lemma
`Finset.mem_filter : a Ôłł s.filter p Ôćö a Ôłł s Ôłž p a`

## Requirements
The predicate `p` must be decidable (`DecidablePred p`).
For predicates involving `<`, `>`, `=`, `%` on natural numbers,
this is automatic.

## Example
```
(Finset.range 10).filter (fun n => n % 2 = 0)
-- = {0, 2, 4, 6, 8}
```
-/
DefinitionDoc Finset.filter as "Finset.filter"

NewDefinition Finset.filter
NewTheorem Finset.mem_filter

DisabledTactic trivial ┬źdecide┬╗ native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto ext
DisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_union_left Finset.mem_union_right Finset.mem_inter_of_mem Finset.mem_of_mem_filter

1Ôćĺimport Game.Metadata
     2Ôćĺ
     3ÔćĺWorld "FinsetOperations"
     4ÔćĺLevel 8
     5Ôćĺ
     6ÔćĺTitle "Extensionality"
     7Ôćĺ
     8ÔćĺIntroduction "
     9Ôćĺ# The ext Tactic: Proving Set Equality Element-wise
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺTwo finsets are equal when they have the same elements:
    12Ôćĺ
    13Ôćĺ$$s = t \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; \\forall x,\\; x \\in s \\;\\leftrightarrow\\; x \\in t$$
    14Ôćĺ
    15ÔćĺThis is `Finset.ext_iff`. The `ext` tactic automates this: on a goal
    16Ôćĺ`s = t` where `s` and `t` are finsets, `ext x` introduces a variable
    17Ôćĺ`x` and changes the goal to `x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t`.
    18Ôćĺ
    19Ôćĺ```
    20Ôćĺext x
    21Ôćĺ-- Goal becomes: x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t
    22Ôćĺ```
    23Ôćĺ
    24ÔćĺThen you prove the biconditional with `constructor`, giving two
    25Ôćĺimplications.
    26Ôćĺ
    27ÔćĺCompare this with `Subset.antisymm` from the previous level:
    28Ôćĺ- `Subset.antisymm` creates two subset goals (`ÔŐć` in both directions)
    29Ôćĺ- `ext` creates one biconditional goal (`Ôćö`)
    30Ôćĺ
    31ÔćĺThey're logically equivalent, but `ext` is more direct.
    32Ôćĺ
    33Ôćĺ**Your task**: Given that every element of `s` is in `t` and vice versa,
    34Ôćĺprove `s = t` using `ext`.
    35Ôćĺ"
    36Ôćĺ
    37Ôćĺ/-- If s and t have the same elements, they are equal. -/
    38ÔćĺStatement (s t : Finset ÔäĽ) (hs : ÔłÇ x, x Ôłł s Ôćĺ x Ôłł t) (ht : ÔłÇ x, x Ôłł t Ôćĺ x Ôłł s) :
    39Ôćĺ    s = t := by
    40Ôćĺ  Hint "Use `ext x` to introduce a variable and convert the
    41Ôćĺ  equality into an element-wise biconditional."
    42Ôćĺ  ext x
    43Ôćĺ  Hint "The goal is `x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t`. Use `constructor` to split
    44Ôćĺ  the biconditional into two implications."
    45Ôćĺ  constructor
    46Ôćĺ  ┬Ě Hint "The goal is `x Ôłł s Ôćĺ x Ôłł t`. You have `hs : ÔłÇ x, x Ôłł s Ôćĺ x Ôłł t`.
    47Ôćĺ    Use `exact hs x` to close it."
    48Ôćĺ    exact hs x
    49Ôćĺ  ┬Ě Hint "The goal is `x Ôłł t Ôćĺ x Ôłł s`. Use `exact ht x`."
    50Ôćĺ    exact ht x
    51Ôćĺ
    52ÔćĺConclusion "
    53ÔćĺThe `ext` tactic is the standard way to prove finset equality in Lean.
    54ÔćĺThe pattern:
    55Ôćĺ
    56Ôćĺ1. `ext x` ÔÇö introduces a variable `x` and converts `s = t` to `x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t`
    57Ôćĺ2. `constructor` ÔÇö splits the biconditional into two implications
    58Ôćĺ3. Prove each direction
    59Ôćĺ
    60ÔćĺIn this level, the hypotheses `hs` and `ht` handed you the
    61Ôćĺimplications directly. In the next level, you'll prove an abstract
    62Ôćĺset identity where you need to do the element-chasing yourself.
    63Ôćĺ
    64ÔćĺThe key insight: *finsets are determined by their elements*. This is
    65Ôćĺthe **extensionality principle** ÔÇö two collections are equal when they
    66Ôćĺhave the same members.
    67Ôćĺ
    68Ôćĺ**Why extensionality?** This is a design choice, not an inevitability.
    69ÔćĺA list like `[1, 1, 2]` is different from `[1, 2]` ÔÇö lists track
    70Ôćĺmultiplicity and order. A *multiset* like `\\{\\!\\{1, 1, 2\\}\\!\\}` differs from
    71Ôćĺ`\\{\\!\\{1, 2\\}\\!\\}` ÔÇö multisets track multiplicity but not order. A finset
    72Ôćĺlike `\\{1, 1, 2\\}` equals `\\{1, 2\\}` ÔÇö finsets track neither multiplicity
    73Ôćĺnor order, only *which elements are present*. Extensionality is what
    74Ôćĺmakes a finset a finset rather than a list or multiset.
    75Ôćĺ
    76Ôćĺ**The unfold-to-logic recipe**: From now on, most set identity proofs
    77Ôćĺwill follow the same three-phase pattern:
    78Ôćĺ1. **ext x** ÔÇö reduce set equality to element-wise logic
    79Ôćĺ2. **Unfold operations** ÔÇö use `rw [mem_union]`, `rw [mem_inter]`, etc.
    80Ôćĺ   (or `simp only [...]`) to convert membership into pure logic
    81Ôćĺ3. **Handle the logic** ÔÇö use `constructor`, `cases`, `left`/`right`,
    82Ôćĺ   `.1`/`.2` to prove the resulting propositional equivalence
    83Ôćĺ
    84ÔćĺWe'll call this the **unfold-to-logic** strategy. Every set identity in
    85Ôćĺthe remaining levels follows this recipe.
    86Ôćĺ"
    87Ôćĺ
    88Ôćĺ/-- `Finset.ext_iff` states that `s = t Ôćö ÔłÇ x, x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t`.
    89Ôćĺ
    90ÔćĺThis is the theorem underlying the `ext` tactic for finsets.
    91Ôćĺ
    92Ôćĺ## Syntax
    93Ôćĺ```
    94Ôćĺrw [Finset.ext_iff]  -- converts s = t to ÔłÇ x, x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t
    95Ôćĺ```
    96Ôćĺ
    97Ôćĺ## When to use it
    98ÔćĺUsually you would use the `ext` tactic instead, which is more
    99Ôćĺconvenient. Use `ext_iff` directly when you need the iff form
   100Ôćĺ(e.g., for rewriting in the reverse direction).
   101Ôćĺ-/
   102ÔćĺTheoremDoc Finset.ext_iff as "Finset.ext_iff" in "Finset"
   103Ôćĺ
   104ÔćĺNewTheorem Finset.ext_iff

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 8

Title "Extensionality"

Introduction "
# The ext Tactic: Proving Set Equality Element-wise

Two finsets are equal when they have the same elements:

$$s = t \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; \\forall x,\\; x \\in s \\;\\leftrightarrow\\; x \\in t$$

This is `Finset.ext_iff`. The `ext` tactic automates this: on a goal
`s = t` where `s` and `t` are finsets, `ext x` introduces a variable
`x` and changes the goal to `x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t`.

```
ext x
-- Goal becomes: x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t
```

Then you prove the biconditional with `constructor`, giving two
implications.

Compare this with `Subset.antisymm` from the previous level:
- `Subset.antisymm` creates two subset goals (`ÔŐć` in both directions)
- `ext` creates one biconditional goal (`Ôćö`)

They're logically equivalent, but `ext` is more direct.

**Your task**: Given that every element of `s` is in `t` and vice versa,
prove `s = t` using `ext`.
"

/-- If s and t have the same elements, they are equal. -/
Statement (s t : Finset ÔäĽ) (hs : ÔłÇ x, x Ôłł s Ôćĺ x Ôłł t) (ht : ÔłÇ x, x Ôłł t Ôćĺ x Ôłł s) :
    s = t := by
  Hint "Use `ext x` to introduce a variable and convert the
  equality into an element-wise biconditional."
  ext x
  Hint "The goal is `x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t`. Use `constructor` to split
  the biconditional into two implications."
  constructor
  ┬Ě Hint "The goal is `x Ôłł s Ôćĺ x Ôłł t`. You have `hs : ÔłÇ x, x Ôłł s Ôćĺ x Ôłł t`.
    Use `exact hs x` to close it."
    exact hs x
  ┬Ě Hint "The goal is `x Ôłł t Ôćĺ x Ôłł s`. Use `exact ht x`."
    exact ht x

Conclusion "
The `ext` tactic is the standard way to prove finset equality in Lean.
The pattern:

1. `ext x` ÔÇö introduces a variable `x` and converts `s = t` to `x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t`
2. `constructor` ÔÇö splits the biconditional into two implications
3. Prove each direction

In this level, the hypotheses `hs` and `ht` handed you the
implications directly. In the next level, you'll prove an abstract
set identity where you need to do the element-chasing yourself.

The key insight: *finsets are determined by their elements*. This is
the **extensionality principle** ÔÇö two collections are equal when they
have the same members.

**Why extensionality?** This is a design choice, not an inevitability.
A list like `[1, 1, 2]` is different from `[1, 2]` ÔÇö lists track
multiplicity and order. A *multiset* like `\\{\\!\\{1, 1, 2\\}\\!\\}` differs from
`\\{\\!\\{1, 2\\}\\!\\}` ÔÇö multisets track multiplicity but not order. A finset
like `\\{1, 1, 2\\}` equals `\\{1, 2\\}` ÔÇö finsets track neither multiplicity
nor order, only *which elements are present*. Extensionality is what
makes a finset a finset rather than a list or multiset.

**The unfold-to-logic recipe**: From now on, most set identity proofs
will follow the same three-phase pattern:
1. **ext x** ÔÇö reduce set equality to element-wise logic
2. **Unfold operations** ÔÇö use `rw [mem_union]`, `rw [mem_inter]`, etc.
   (or `simp only [...]`) to convert membership into pure logic
3. **Handle the logic** ÔÇö use `constructor`, `cases`, `left`/`right`,
   `.1`/`.2` to prove the resulting propositional equivalence

We'll call this the **unfold-to-logic** strategy. Every set identity in
the remaining levels follows this recipe.
"

/-- `Finset.ext_iff` states that `s = t Ôćö ÔłÇ x, x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t`.

This is the theorem underlying the `ext` tactic for finsets.

## Syntax
```
rw [Finset.ext_iff]  -- converts s = t to ÔłÇ x, x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t
```

## When to use it
Usually you would use the `ext` tactic instead, which is more
convenient. Use `ext_iff` directly when you need the iff form
(e.g., for rewriting in the reverse direction).
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.ext_iff as "Finset.ext_iff" in "Finset"

NewTheorem Finset.ext_iff

DisabledTactic trivial ┬źdecide┬╗ native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
DisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_union_left Finset.mem_union_right Finset.mem_inter_of_mem Finset.subset_union_left Finset.subset_union_right Finset.inter_subset_left Finset.inter_subset_right Finset.union_comm Finset.inter_comm sup_comm inf_comm

1Ôćĺimport Game.Metadata
     2Ôćĺ
     3ÔćĺWorld "PsetFin"
     4ÔćĺLevel 17
     5Ôćĺ
     6ÔćĺTitle "The Pigeonhole Principle (Concrete)"
     7Ôćĺ
     8ÔćĺIntroduction "
     9Ôćĺ# Three Pigeons, Two Holes
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺThe **pigeonhole principle** says: if you put more objects into
    12Ôćĺfewer boxes, at least two objects share a box.
    13Ôćĺ
    14ÔćĺFormally: any function `f : Fin 3 -> Fin 2` must send two
    15Ôćĺdifferent inputs to the same output. There are 3 inputs but
    16Ôćĺonly 2 possible outputs, so a collision is inevitable.
    17Ôćĺ
    18ÔćĺThis is your first real **theorem from combinatorics** ÔÇö and
    19Ôćĺyou can prove it using only the tools from Phase 1.
    20Ôćĺ
    21Ôćĺ**Strategy**:
    22Ôćĺ1. Classify each `Fin 2` value: every `x : Fin 2` is either `0` or `1`
    23Ôćĺ2. Apply the classification to `f 0` and `f 1`
    24Ôćĺ3. If they're equal: witnesses are `0` and `1`
    25Ôćĺ4. If they differ: check `f 2` ÔÇö it must match one of them
    26Ôćĺ"
    27Ôćĺ
    28Ôćĺ/-- Any function from Fin 3 to Fin 2 has a collision. -/
    29ÔćĺStatement (f : Fin 3 Ôćĺ Fin 2) : Ôłâ i j : Fin 3, i Ôëá j Ôłž f i = f j := by
    30Ôćĺ  -- Helper: every Fin 2 element is 0 or 1
    31Ôćĺ  Hint "First, prove a helper: every element of `Fin 2` is `0` or `1`.
    32Ôćĺ  Use `have classify : forall x : Fin 2, x = 0 or x = 1 := by ...`"
    33Ôćĺ  Hint (hidden := true) "Inside the `have`, destructure:
    34Ôćĺ  `intro (v, hv); cases v with | zero => left; rfl | succ n => ...`
    35Ôćĺ  For `succ n`: `cases n with | zero => right; rfl | succ m => exact absurd hv (by omega)`"
    36Ôćĺ  have classify : ÔłÇ x : Fin 2, x = 0 ÔłĘ x = 1 := by
    37Ôćĺ    intro ÔčĘv, hvÔčę
    38Ôćĺ    cases v with
    39Ôćĺ    | zero => left; rfl
    40Ôćĺ    | succ n =>
    41Ôćĺ      cases n with
    42Ôćĺ      | zero => right; rfl
    43Ôćĺ      | succ m => exact absurd hv (by omega)
    44Ôćĺ  Hint "Now classify `f 0` and `f 1`:
    45Ôćĺ  `have h0 := classify (f 0)` and `have h1 := classify (f 1)`."
    46Ôćĺ  have h0 := classify (f 0)
    47Ôćĺ  have h1 := classify (f 1)
    48Ôćĺ  Hint "Case-split on `h0` and `h1`. If both are the same value,
    49Ôćĺ  you have a collision at indices 0 and 1. If they differ, check `f 2`."
    50Ôćĺ  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases h0 with | inl h0 => ... | inr h0 => ...`
    51Ôćĺ  then inside each branch, `cases h1 with | inl h1 => ... | inr h1 => ...`"
    52Ôćĺ  cases h0 with
    53Ôćĺ  | inl h0 =>
    54Ôćĺ    cases h1 with
    55Ôćĺ    | inl h1 =>
    56Ôćĺ      -- f 0 = 0, f 1 = 0: collision at 0 and 1
    57Ôćĺ      Hint "Both `f 0` and `f 1` map to `0` ÔÇö collision at indices 0 and 1.
    58Ôćĺ      Provide the witnesses and prove the conjunction."
    59Ôćĺ      Hint (hidden := true) "`use 0, 1; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h0, h1]`"
    60Ôćĺ      use 0, 1
    61Ôćĺ      constructor
    62Ôćĺ      ┬Ě intro h; cases h
    63Ôćĺ      ┬Ě rw [h0, h1]
    64Ôćĺ    | inr h1 =>
    65Ôćĺ      -- f 0 = 0, f 1 = 1: check f 2
    66Ôćĺ      Hint "The outputs differ (`f 0 = 0`, `f 1 = 1`), so no collision
    67Ôćĺ      yet. Check `f 2` ÔÇö it must equal `0` or `1`, matching one of them."
    68Ôćĺ      Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 := classify (f 2)` then case-split on `h2`."
    69Ôćĺ      have h2 := classify (f 2)
    70Ôćĺ      cases h2 with
    71Ôćĺ      | inl h2 =>
    72Ôćĺ        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 0 = f 0`: use `0, 2; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h0, h2]`"
    73Ôćĺ        use 0, 2
    74Ôćĺ        constructor
    75Ôćĺ        ┬Ě intro h; cases h
    76Ôćĺ        ┬Ě rw [h0, h2]
    77Ôćĺ      | inr h2 =>
    78Ôćĺ        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 1 = f 1`: use `1, 2; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h1, h2]`"
    79Ôćĺ        use 1, 2
    80Ôćĺ        constructor
    81Ôćĺ        ┬Ě intro h; cases h
    82Ôćĺ        ┬Ě rw [h1, h2]
    83Ôćĺ  | inr h0 =>
    84Ôćĺ    cases h1 with
    85Ôćĺ    | inr h1 =>
    86Ôćĺ      -- f 0 = 1, f 1 = 1: collision at 0 and 1
    87Ôćĺ      Hint "Both `f 0` and `f 1` map to `1` ÔÇö collision at indices 0 and 1."
    88Ôćĺ      Hint (hidden := true) "`use 0, 1; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h0, h1]`"
    89Ôćĺ      use 0, 1
    90Ôćĺ      constructor
    91Ôćĺ      ┬Ě intro h; cases h
    92Ôćĺ      ┬Ě rw [h0, h1]
    93Ôćĺ    | inl h1 =>
    94Ôćĺ      -- f 0 = 1, f 1 = 0: check f 2
    95Ôćĺ      Hint "The outputs differ (`f 0 = 1`, `f 1 = 0`), so no collision
    96Ôćĺ      yet. Check `f 2` ÔÇö it must match one of them."
    97Ôćĺ      Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 := classify (f 2)` then case-split on `h2`."
    98Ôćĺ      have h2 := classify (f 2)
    99Ôćĺ      cases h2 with
   100Ôćĺ      | inl h2 =>
   101Ôćĺ        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 0 = f 1`: use `1, 2; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h1, h2]`"
   102Ôćĺ        use 1, 2
   103Ôćĺ        constructor
   104Ôćĺ        ┬Ě intro h; cases h
   105Ôćĺ        ┬Ě rw [h1, h2]
   106Ôćĺ      | inr h2 =>
   107Ôćĺ        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 1 = f 0`: use `0, 2; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h0, h2]`"
   108Ôćĺ        use 0, 2
   109Ôćĺ        constructor
   110Ôćĺ        ┬Ě intro h; cases h
   111Ôćĺ        ┬Ě rw [h0, h2]
   112Ôćĺ
   113ÔćĺConclusion "
   114ÔćĺYou've proved the **pigeonhole principle** for `Fin 3 -> Fin 2`:
   115Ôćĺany function from a 3-element set to a 2-element set must have
   116Ôćĺa collision.
   117Ôćĺ
   118ÔćĺThe proof strategy was:
   119Ôćĺ1. **Classify** each output as `0` or `1`
   120Ôćĺ2. **Compare** outputs at pairs of inputs
   121Ôćĺ3. **Find** two inputs with the same output
   122Ôćĺ
   123ÔćĺEvery tool in this proof came from Phase 1:
   124Ôćĺ- Case analysis on `Fin 2` (MeetFin)
   125Ôćĺ- Existential witnesses with `use` (MeetFin)
   126Ôćĺ- Inequality pattern `intro h; cases h` (MeetFin)
   127Ôćĺ- Rewriting with hypotheses (FinTuples)
   128Ôćĺ
   129Ôćĺ**The cardinality consequence**: This result tells us something
   130Ôćĺfundamental about `Fin n` as a type. If there were an injection
   131Ôćĺfrom `Fin 3` to `Fin 2` (a function with no collision), we could
   132Ôćĺembed a 3-element set into a 2-element set ÔÇö which is impossible.
   133ÔćĺSo `|Fin 3| > |Fin 2|`, confirming that `Fin 3` is genuinely
   134Ôćĺ'larger than' `Fin 2`. More importantly, the parameter `n` in
   135Ôćĺ`Fin n` is *recoverable* from the type: `Fin n` and `Fin m` can
   136Ôćĺonly be equivalent (have a bijection between them) when `n = m`.
   137ÔćĺThis is what makes `Fin n` the canonical finite type ÔÇö it uniquely
   138Ôćĺrepresents 'a type with exactly n elements.'
   139Ôćĺ
   140ÔćĺThe general pigeonhole principle ÔÇö if `f : Fin (n+1) -> Fin n`,
   141Ôćĺthen `f` has a collision ÔÇö is a cornerstone of combinatorics.
   142ÔćĺYou'll prove the general version in a later world with more
   143Ôćĺpowerful tools. This concrete version demonstrates that your
   144ÔćĺPhase 1 toolkit can already prove real theorems.
   145Ôćĺ"
   146Ôćĺ
   147ÔćĺDisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases
   148ÔćĺDisabledTheorem funext

file.content:
import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFin"
Level 17

Title "The Pigeonhole Principle (Concrete)"

Introduction "
# Three Pigeons, Two Holes

The **pigeonhole principle** says: if you put more objects into
fewer boxes, at least two objects share a box.

Formally: any function `f : Fin 3 -> Fin 2` must send two
different inputs to the same output. There are 3 inputs but
only 2 possible outputs, so a collision is inevitable.

This is your first real **theorem from combinatorics** ÔÇö and
you can prove it using only the tools from Phase 1.

**Strategy**:
1. Classify each `Fin 2` value: every `x : Fin 2` is either `0` or `1`
2. Apply the classification to `f 0` and `f 1`
3. If they're equal: witnesses are `0` and `1`
4. If they differ: check `f 2` ÔÇö it must match one of them
"

/-- Any function from Fin 3 to Fin 2 has a collision. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 Ôćĺ Fin 2) : Ôłâ i j : Fin 3, i Ôëá j Ôłž f i = f j := by
  -- Helper: every Fin 2 element is 0 or 1
  Hint "First, prove a helper: every element of `Fin 2` is `0` or `1`.
  Use `have classify : forall x : Fin 2, x = 0 or x = 1 := by ...`"
  Hint (hidden := true) "Inside the `have`, destructure:
  `intro (v, hv); cases v with | zero => left; rfl | succ n => ...`
  For `succ n`: `cases n with | zero => right; rfl | succ m => exact absurd hv (by omega)`"
  have classify : ÔłÇ x : Fin 2, x = 0 ÔłĘ x = 1 := by
    intro ÔčĘv, hvÔčę
    cases v with
    | zero => left; rfl
    | succ n =>
      cases n with
      | zero => right; rfl
      | succ m => exact absurd hv (by omega)
  Hint "Now classify `f 0` and `f 1`:
  `have h0 := classify (f 0)` and `have h1 := classify (f 1)`."
  have h0 := classify (f 0)
  have h1 := classify (f 1)
  Hint "Case-split on `h0` and `h1`. If both are the same value,
  you have a collision at indices 0 and 1. If they differ, check `f 2`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases h0 with | inl h0 => ... | inr h0 => ...`
  then inside each branch, `cases h1 with | inl h1 => ... | inr h1 => ...`"
  cases h0 with
  | inl h0 =>
    cases h1 with
    | inl h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 0, f 1 = 0: collision at 0 and 1
      Hint "Both `f 0` and `f 1` map to `0` ÔÇö collision at indices 0 and 1.
      Provide the witnesses and prove the conjunction."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`use 0, 1; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h0, h1]`"
      use 0, 1
      constructor
      ┬Ě intro h; cases h
      ┬Ě rw [h0, h1]
    | inr h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 0, f 1 = 1: check f 2
      Hint "The outputs differ (`f 0 = 0`, `f 1 = 1`), so no collision
      yet. Check `f 2` ÔÇö it must equal `0` or `1`, matching one of them."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 := classify (f 2)` then case-split on `h2`."
      have h2 := classify (f 2)
      cases h2 with
      | inl h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 0 = f 0`: use `0, 2; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h0, h2]`"
        use 0, 2
        constructor
        ┬Ě intro h; cases h
        ┬Ě rw [h0, h2]
      | inr h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 1 = f 1`: use `1, 2; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h1, h2]`"
        use 1, 2
        constructor
        ┬Ě intro h; cases h
        ┬Ě rw [h1, h2]
  | inr h0 =>
    cases h1 with
    | inr h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 1, f 1 = 1: collision at 0 and 1
      Hint "Both `f 0` and `f 1` map to `1` ÔÇö collision at indices 0 and 1."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`use 0, 1; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h0, h1]`"
      use 0, 1
      constructor
      ┬Ě intro h; cases h
      ┬Ě rw [h0, h1]
    | inl h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 1, f 1 = 0: check f 2
      Hint "The outputs differ (`f 0 = 1`, `f 1 = 0`), so no collision
      yet. Check `f 2` ÔÇö it must match one of them."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 := classify (f 2)` then case-split on `h2`."
      have h2 := classify (f 2)
      cases h2 with
      | inl h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 0 = f 1`: use `1, 2; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h1, h2]`"
        use 1, 2
        constructor
        ┬Ě intro h; cases h
        ┬Ě rw [h1, h2]
      | inr h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 1 = f 0`: use `0, 2; constructor; ┬Ě intro h; cases h; ┬Ě rw [h0, h2]`"
        use 0, 2
        constructor
        ┬Ě intro h; cases h
        ┬Ě rw [h0, h2]

Conclusion "
You've proved the **pigeonhole principle** for `Fin 3 -> Fin 2`:
any function from a 3-element set to a 2-element set must have
a collision.

The proof strategy was:
1. **Classify** each output as `0` or `1`
2. **Compare** outputs at pairs of inputs
3. **Find** two inputs with the same output

Every tool in this proof came from Phase 1:
- Case analysis on `Fin 2` (MeetFin)
- Existential witnesses with `use` (MeetFin)
- Inequality pattern `intro h; cases h` (MeetFin)
- Rewriting with hypotheses (FinTuples)

**The cardinality consequence**: This result tells us something
fundamental about `Fin n` as a type. If there were an injection
from `Fin 3` to `Fin 2` (a function with no collision), we could
embed a 3-element set into a 2-element set ÔÇö which is impossible.
So `|Fin 3| > |Fin 2|`, confirming that `Fin 3` is genuinely
'larger than' `Fin 2`. More importantly, the parameter `n` in
`Fin n` is *recoverable* from the type: `Fin n` and `Fin m` can
only be equivalent (have a bijection between them) when `n = m`.
This is what makes `Fin n` the canonical finite type ÔÇö it uniquely
represents 'a type with exactly n elements.'

The general pigeonhole principle ÔÇö if `f : Fin (n+1) -> Fin n`,
then `f` has a collision ÔÇö is a cornerstone of combinatorics.
You'll prove the general version in a later world with more
powerful tools. This concrete version demonstrates that your
Phase 1 toolkit can already prove real theorems.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFin"
Level 16

Title "The Cyclic Permutation Has Order 3"

Introduction "
# Proving Order 3 Directly

In Level 15, you proved `![1,2,0]^2 = ![2,0,1]` and that
`![2,0,1]` is the inverse (composing it with `![1,2,0]` gives `id`).

Now prove the punchline directly: applying `![1,2,0]` **three
times** returns to the identity.

The composition `![1,2,0] comp ![1,2,0] comp ![1,2,0]` evaluates as:
- At 0: `0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0`
- At 1: `1 -> 2 -> 0 -> 1`
- At 2: `2 -> 0 -> 1 -> 2`

Every element returns to itself after three steps.

**Strategy**: `ext`, then `rw [Function.comp_apply, Function.comp_apply]`
to unfold both layers of composition, then case-split and close with `rfl`.
"

/-- The cyclic permutation has order 3: three applications give the identity. -/
Statement : (![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 Ôćĺ Fin 3) Ôłś (![1, 2, 0]) Ôłś (![1, 2, 0]) = id := by
  Hint "Use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality."
  ext ÔčĘv, hvÔčę
  Hint "Unfold both layers of composition:
  `rw [Function.comp_apply, Function.comp_apply]`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Function.comp_apply, Function.comp_apply]`"
  rw [Function.comp_apply, Function.comp_apply]
  Hint "Case-split on `v` and close each case with `rfl`."
  cases v with
  | zero =>
    Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl` ÔÇö `![1,2,0] (![1,2,0] (![1,2,0] 0)) = ![1,2,0] (![1,2,0] 1) = ![1,2,0] 2 = 0`."
    rfl
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl`"
      rfl
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`rfl`"
        rfl
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
The cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0]` has **order 3**: three
applications return to the identity.

Across Levels 14-16, you've built a complete picture of this
permutation:
- **No fixed points** (L14): it's a derangement
- **Square and inverse** (L15): `![1,2,0]^2 = ![2,0,1]`, and
  `![2,0,1]` is the inverse
- **Order 3** (this level): `![1,2,0]^3 = id`

These three facts ÔÇö the permutation, its square, and the identity ÔÇö
form a **cyclic group of order 3**. This is the smallest non-trivial
group, and it appears naturally from the cyclic permutation on three
elements. You've seen the group structure emerge from concrete
computation.

The concept of *order* ÔÇö how many times you must apply an operation
before returning to the starting point ÔÇö is fundamental in abstract
algebra. Every finite group element has a finite order, and the
order divides the size of the group (Lagrange's theorem).

In Mathlib, permutations are formalized as `Equiv.Perm alpha` ÔÇö an
equivalence (bijection) from `alpha` to itself. The cyclic permutation
you've been studying would be an element of `Equiv.Perm (Fin 3)`. The
composition, inverse, and order you computed are exactly the group
operations on `Equiv.Perm`. If you continue to the algebraic_structures
course, you'll work with `Equiv.Perm` directly.
"

1Ôćĺimport Game.Metadata
     2Ôćĺ
     3ÔćĺWorld "FinsetImage"
     4ÔćĺLevel 18
     5Ôćĺ
     6ÔćĺTitle "Size Preservation Implies Injectivity"
     7Ôćĺ
     8ÔćĺIntroduction "
     9Ôćĺ# The Converse: A Finite-Math Equivalence
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺLevel 13 proved that injective functions preserve cardinality:
    12Ôćĺ
    13Ôćĺ$$f \\text{ injective} \\implies |f(S)| = |S|$$
    14Ôćĺ
    15ÔćĺThis level addresses the **converse**: if $|f(S)| = |S|$, must $f$
    16Ôćĺbe injective on $S$?
    17Ôćĺ
    18ÔćĺFor finite sets, the answer is **yes**. This is a deep fact about
    19Ôćĺfiniteness: if a function doesn't lose any elements (same cardinality),
    20Ôćĺit can't have any collisions (must be injective). In Mathlib:
    21Ôćĺ
    22Ôćĺ```
    23ÔćĺFinset.card_image_iff : (s.image f).card = s.card Ôćö Set.InjOn f ÔćĹs
    24Ôćĺ```
    25Ôćĺ
    26Ôćĺ`Set.InjOn f ÔćĹs` means `f` is injective when restricted to `s`:
    27Ôćĺfor all `a Ôłł s` and `b Ôłł s`, if `f a = f b` then `a = b`.
    28Ôćĺ
    29Ôćĺ**Your task**: Given that the image preserves cardinality (`hcard`),
    30Ôćĺand two elements `a, b Ôłł s` with `f a = f b`, prove `a = b`.
    31Ôćĺ
    32ÔćĺStrategy: convert `hcard` from a cardinality equation to an
    33Ôćĺinjectivity statement using `Finset.card_image_iff`, then apply it.
    34Ôćĺ"
    35Ôćĺ
    36Ôćĺ/-- If card is preserved, equal outputs imply equal inputs. -/
    37ÔćĺStatement (f : ÔäĽ Ôćĺ ÔäĽ) (s : Finset ÔäĽ)
    38Ôćĺ    (hcard : (s.image f).card = s.card)
    39Ôćĺ    (a b : ÔäĽ) (ha : a Ôłł s) (hb : b Ôłł s) (heq : f a = f b) :
    40Ôćĺ    a = b := by
    41Ôćĺ  Hint "The key step: convert `hcard` from a cardinality equation
    42Ôćĺ  to an injectivity statement.
    43Ôćĺ
    44Ôćĺ  Use `rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard` to transform
    45Ôćĺ  `hcard : (s.image f).card = s.card`
    46Ôćĺ  into
    47Ôćĺ  `hcard : Set.InjOn f ÔćĹs`."
    48Ôćĺ  rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard
    49Ôćĺ  Hint "Now `hcard : Set.InjOn f ÔćĹs`, which means
    50Ôćĺ  `ÔłÇ a Ôłł s, ÔłÇ b Ôłł s, f a = f b Ôćĺ a = b`.
    51Ôćĺ
    52Ôćĺ  You have `ha : a Ôłł s`, `hb : b Ôłł s`, and `heq : f a = f b`.
    53Ôćĺ  Apply `hcard` to these: `exact hcard ha hb heq`."
    54Ôćĺ  exact hcard ha hb heq
    55Ôćĺ
    56ÔćĺConclusion "
    57ÔćĺThis is the **fundamental equivalence** of finite-set images:
    58Ôćĺ
    59Ôćĺ$$|f(S)| = |S| \\iff f \\text{ is injective on } S$$
    60Ôćĺ
    61ÔćĺThe forward direction ($\\Leftarrow$) is Level 13's
    62Ôćĺ`card_image_of_injective`. The backward direction ($\\Rightarrow$)
    63Ôćĺis the converse you just used.
    64Ôćĺ
    65ÔćĺThis equivalence is **unique to finite sets** ÔÇö for infinite sets,
    66Ôćĺa function can preserve 'size' (cardinality) while not being
    67Ôćĺinjective (e.g., $n \\mapsto \\lfloor n/2 \\rfloor$ maps $\\mathbb{N}$
    68Ôćĺonto $\\mathbb{N}$ but is not injective).
    69Ôćĺ
    70ÔćĺThe equivalence connects several themes:
    71Ôćĺ- **Pigeonhole principle** (PsetFin): if you map $n+1$ pigeons
    72Ôćĺ  into $n$ holes, two must collide ÔÇö contrapositive of the
    73Ôćĺ  converse direction
    74Ôćĺ- **Counting arguments**: size preservation is a test for
    75Ôćĺ  injectivity without checking every pair
    76Ôćĺ- **Finite type theory**: `Fintype.card_congr` will generalize
    77Ôćĺ  this from `Finset` to `Fintype`
    78Ôćĺ
    79ÔćĺIn the next level, you'll see this equivalence in action: using
    80Ôćĺcardinality to prove injectivity for a function where checking
    81Ôćĺevery pair directly would be tedious. The backward direction
    82Ôćĺ($\\Rightarrow$) is especially powerful: it lets you prove
    83Ôćĺinjectivity by counting, avoiding the need to check every pair
    84Ôćĺof inputs.
    85Ôćĺ
    86ÔćĺYou'll use `card_image_iff` again in the Cardinality world,
    87Ôćĺwhere counting arguments routinely convert between cardinality
    88Ôćĺequations and injectivity statements.
    89Ôćĺ"
    90Ôćĺ
    91Ôćĺ/-- `Finset.card_image_iff` states that
    92Ôćĺ`(s.image f).card = s.card Ôćö Set.InjOn f ÔćĹs`.
    93Ôćĺ
    94ÔćĺThe image of a finset preserves cardinality if and only if the
    95Ôćĺfunction is injective on the set.
    96Ôćĺ
    97Ôćĺ## Syntax
    98Ôćĺ```
    99Ôćĺrw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard  -- convert card eq to injectivity
   100Ôćĺ```
   101Ôćĺ
   102Ôćĺ## When to use it
   103ÔćĺWhen you need to convert between a cardinality equation and an
   104Ôćĺinjectivity statement on a finset.

   107Ôćĺ
   108Ôćĺ/-- `Set.InjOn f s` means `f` is injective when restricted to `s`:
   109Ôćĺfor all `a Ôłł s` and `b Ôłł s`, `f a = f b Ôćĺ a = b`.
   110Ôćĺ
   111Ôćĺ## Definition
   112Ôćĺ```
   113ÔćĺSet.InjOn f s := ÔłÇ a Ôłł s, ÔłÇ b Ôłł s, f a = f b Ôćĺ a = b
   114Ôćĺ```
   115Ôćĺ
   116Ôćĺ## Relationship to `Function.Injective`
   117Ôćĺ- `Function.Injective f` is global: for ALL `a b`.
   118Ôćĺ- `Set.InjOn f s` is local: only for `a b Ôłł s`.

file.content:
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetImage"
Level 18

Title "Size Preservation Implies Injectivity"

Introduction "
# The Converse: A Finite-Math Equivalence

Level 13 proved that injective functions preserve cardinality:

$$f \\text{ injective} \\implies |f(S)| = |S|$$

This level addresses the **converse**: if $|f(S)| = |S|$, must $f$
be injective on $S$?

For finite sets, the answer is **yes**. This is a deep fact about
finiteness: if a function doesn't lose any elements (same cardinality),
it can't have any collisions (must be injective). In Mathlib:

```
Finset.card_image_iff : (s.image f).card = s.card Ôćö Set.InjOn f ÔćĹs
```

`Set.InjOn f ÔćĹs` means `f` is injective when restricted to `s`:
for all `a Ôłł s` and `b Ôłł s`, if `f a = f b` then `a = b`.

**Your task**: Given that the image preserves cardinality (`hcard`),
and two elements `a, b Ôłł s` with `f a = f b`, prove `a = b`.

Strategy: convert `hcard` from a cardinality equation to an
injectivity statement using `Finset.card_image_iff`, then apply it.
"

/-- If card is preserved, equal outputs imply equal inputs. -/
Statement (f : ÔäĽ Ôćĺ ÔäĽ) (s : Finset ÔäĽ)
    (hcard : (s.image f).card = s.card)
    (a b : ÔäĽ) (ha : a Ôłł s) (hb : b Ôłł s) (heq : f a = f b) :
    a = b := by
  Hint "The key step: convert `hcard` from a cardinality equation
  to an injectivity statement.

  Use `rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard` to transform
  `hcard : (s.image f).card = s.card`
  into
  `hcard : Set.InjOn f ÔćĹs`."
  rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard
  Hint "Now `hcard : Set.InjOn f ÔćĹs`, which means
  `ÔłÇ a Ôłł s, ÔłÇ b Ôłł s, f a = f b Ôćĺ a = b`.

  You have `ha : a Ôłł s`, `hb : b Ôłł s`, and `heq : f a = f b`.
  Apply `hcard` to these: `exact hcard ha hb heq`."
  exact hcard ha hb heq

Conclusion "
This is the **fundamental equivalence** of finite-set images:

$$|f(S)| = |S| \\iff f \\text{ is injective on } S$$

The forward direction ($\\Leftarrow$) is Level 13's
`card_image_of_injective`. The backward direction ($\\Rightarrow$)
is the converse you just used.

This equivalence is **unique to finite sets** ÔÇö for infinite sets,
a function can preserve 'size' (cardinality) while not being
injective (e.g., $n \\mapsto \\lfloor n/2 \\rfloor$ maps $\\mathbb{N}$
onto $\\mathbb{N}$ but is not injective).

The equivalence connects several themes:
- **Pigeonhole principle** (PsetFin): if you map $n+1$ pigeons
  into $n$ holes, two must collide ÔÇö contrapositive of the
  converse direction
- **Counting arguments**: size preservation is a test for
  injectivity without checking every pair
- **Finite type theory**: `Fintype.card_congr` will generalize
  this from `Finset` to `Fintype`

In the next level, you'll see this equivalence in action: using
cardinality to prove injectivity for a function where checking
every pair directly would be tedious. The backward direction
($\\Rightarrow$) is especially powerful: it lets you prove
injectivity by counting, avoiding the need to check every pair
of inputs.

You'll use `card_image_iff` again in the Cardinality world,
where counting arguments routinely convert between cardinality
equations and injectivity statements.
"

/-- `Finset.card_image_iff` states that
`(s.image f).card = s.card Ôćö Set.InjOn f ÔćĹs`.

The image of a finset preserves cardinality if and only if the
function is injective on the set.

## Syntax
```
rw [Finset.card_image_iff] at hcard  -- convert card eq to injectivity
```

## When to use it
When you need to convert between a cardinality equation and an
injectivity statement on a finset.
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.card_image_iff as "Finset.card_image_iff" in "Finset"

/-- `Set.InjOn f s` means `f` is injective when restricted to `s`:
for all `a Ôłł s` and `b Ôłł s`, `f a = f b Ôćĺ a = b`.

## Definition
```
Set.InjOn f s := ÔłÇ a Ôłł s, ÔłÇ b Ôłł s, f a = f b Ôćĺ a = b
```

## Relationship to `Function.Injective`
- `Function.Injective f` is global: for ALL `a b`.
- `Set.InjOn f s` is local: only for `a b Ôłł s`.
- Every injective function is injective on any set.
-/
DefinitionDoc Set.InjOn as "Set.InjOn"

NewDefinition Set.InjOn
NewTheorem Finset.card_image_iff

DisabledTactic trivial ┬źdecide┬╗ native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto push_neg linarith
DisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_image_of_mem Finset.image_subset_image Finset.image_mono

1Ôćĺimport Game.Metadata
     2Ôćĺ
     3ÔćĺWorld "FinsetImage"
     4ÔćĺLevel 19
     5Ôćĺ
     6ÔćĺTitle "Counting Proves Injectivity"
     7Ôćĺ
     8ÔćĺIntroduction "
     9Ôćĺ# The Payoff: Cardinality as a Proof Technique
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺLevel 17 gave you `Finset.card_image_iff`:
    12Ôćĺ
    13Ôćĺ$$|f(S)| = |S| \\iff f \\text{ is injective on } S$$
    14Ôćĺ
    15ÔćĺNow you'll use this as a **proof tool**. Consider
    16Ôćĺ$f(n) = n \\cdot n + 1$ on $S = \\{0, 1, 2\\}$:
    17Ôćĺ
    18Ôćĺ| $n$ | $f(n) = n^2 + 1$ |
    19Ôćĺ|---|---|
    20Ôćĺ| 0 | 1 |
    21Ôćĺ| 1 | 2 |
    22Ôćĺ| 2 | 5 |
    23Ôćĺ
    24ÔćĺThe image $\\{1, 2, 5\\}$ has 3 elements, the same as $S$. By
    25Ôćĺ`card_image_iff`, this means $f$ is injective on $S$.
    26Ôćĺ
    27ÔćĺProving injectivity directly for a quadratic function requires
    28Ôćĺchecking that $a^2 + 1 = b^2 + 1$ implies $a = b$ for all pairs
    29Ôćĺin $S$ ÔÇö tedious. But counting the image is immediate: same
    30Ôćĺsize means no collisions.
    31Ôćĺ
    32Ôćĺ**Your task**: Prove that $f$ is injective on $S$ by converting
    33Ôćĺthe goal to a cardinality equation, then letting Lean verify
    34Ôćĺthe computation.
    35Ôćĺ
    36ÔćĺStrategy:
    37Ôćĺ1. `rw [ÔćÉ Finset.card_image_iff]` ÔÇö convert the injectivity
    38Ôćĺ   goal into a cardinality equation
    39Ôćĺ2. `native_decide` ÔÇö let Lean compute both sides and verify equality
    40Ôćĺ"
    41Ôćĺ
    42Ôćĺ/-- Counting proves injectivity: n ÔćŽ n┬▓ + 1 is injective on {0, 1, 2}. -/
    43ÔćĺStatement :
    44Ôćĺ    Set.InjOn (fun n : ÔäĽ => n * n + 1) ÔćĹ({0, 1, 2} : Finset ÔäĽ) := by
    45Ôćĺ  Hint "Convert the injectivity goal into a cardinality equation.
    46Ôćĺ  Use `rw [ÔćÉ Finset.card_image_iff]` ÔÇö the `ÔćÉ` rewrites the goal

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetImage"
Level 19

Title "Counting Proves Injectivity"

Introduction "
# The Payoff: Cardinality as a Proof Technique

Level 17 gave you `Finset.card_image_iff`:

$$|f(S)| = |S| \\iff f \\text{ is injective on } S$$

Now you'll use this as a **proof tool**. Consider
$f(n) = n \\cdot n + 1$ on $S = \\{0, 1, 2\\}$:

| $n$ | $f(n) = n^2 + 1$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 1 |
| 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 5 |

The image $\\{1, 2, 5\\}$ has 3 elements, the same as $S$. By
`card_image_iff`, this means $f$ is injective on $S$.

Proving injectivity directly for a quadratic function requires
checking that $a^2 + 1 = b^2 + 1$ implies $a = b$ for all pairs
in $S$ ÔÇö tedious. But counting the image is immediate: same
size means no collisions.

**Your task**: Prove that $f$ is injective on $S$ by converting
the goal to a cardinality equation, then letting Lean verify
the computation.

Strategy:
1. `rw [ÔćÉ Finset.card_image_iff]` ÔÇö convert the injectivity
   goal into a cardinality equation
2. `native_decide` ÔÇö let Lean compute both sides and verify equality
"

/-- Counting proves injectivity: n ÔćŽ n┬▓ + 1 is injective on {0, 1, 2}. -/
Statement :
    Set.InjOn (fun n : ÔäĽ => n * n + 1) ÔćĹ({0, 1, 2} : Finset ÔäĽ) := by
  Hint "Convert the injectivity goal into a cardinality equation.
  Use `rw [ÔćÉ Finset.card_image_iff]` ÔÇö the `ÔćÉ` rewrites the goal
  from `Set.InjOn ...` to `(s.image f).card = s.card`."
  rw [ÔćÉ Finset.card_image_iff]
  Hint "The goal is now a concrete cardinality equation. Lean can
  compute both sides: the image is `(1, 2, 5)` with 3 elements,
  and the input has 3 elements. Use `native_decide` to let Lean
  verify this computationally."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `native_decide`."
  native_decide

Conclusion "
You just proved injectivity **without checking a single pair**.
Instead of showing $a^2 + 1 = b^2 + 1 \\implies a = b$ for all
$a, b \\in \\{0, 1, 2\\}$, you showed $|f(S)| = |S|$ and let
`card_image_iff` do the rest.

This is the counting-proves-injectivity technique:
1. `rw [ÔćÉ Finset.card_image_iff]` ÔÇö convert to cardinality
2. `native_decide` (or `decide`) ÔÇö verify computationally

The technique works whenever the domain is a concrete finset.
For abstract finsets, you'd need to compute or bound the
cardinality by other means.

This is the **payoff** of the cardinality arc (Levels 12-17):
cardinality isn't just a number ÔÇö it's a proof technique. Knowing
$|f(S)| = |S|$ tells you the function has no collisions, which is
the *definition* of injectivity.

Next: the boss, which integrates forward membership, backward
extraction, cardinality bounds, and injectivity into one problem.
"

NewTactic native_decide

-- Note: native_decide is intentionally enabled for this level
-- (not in DisabledTactic) since the level teaches computational verification.
DisabledTactic trivial ┬źdecide┬╗ simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto push_neg linarith
DisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_image_of_mem Finset.image_subset_image Finset.image_mono

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetBasics"
Level 9

Title "The Fin-to-Finset Bridge"

Introduction "
# Connecting Fin to Finset.range

In Phase 1, every element `i : Fin n` carried a proof `i.isLt : i.val < n`.
In Levels 7-8, you proved that `i Ôłł Finset.range n Ôćö i < n`.

These are the same condition! Every `Fin n` element's value is automatically
in `Finset.range n`. This is the concrete bridge between Phase 1 (indexed
access) and Phase 2 (set membership).

**Your task**: Prove that for any `i : Fin n`, the value `i.val` belongs
to `Finset.range n`.

**Strategy**: Use `rw [Finset.mem_range]` to convert to `i.val < n`,
then use `exact i.isLt` ÔÇö the bound proof that every `Fin n` element
carries.
"

/-- Every Fin n element's value belongs to Finset.range n. -/
Statement (n : ÔäĽ) (i : Fin n) : i.val Ôłł Finset.range n := by
  Hint "Convert the membership goal to an inequality:
  `rw [Finset.mem_range]`."
  rw [Finset.mem_range]
  Hint "The goal is `i.val < n`. Every element of `Fin n` carries
  exactly this proof ÔÇö it's called `i.isLt`. Use `exact i.isLt`."
  exact i.isLt

Conclusion "
This level makes the Phase 1 Ôćĺ Phase 2 connection concrete:

| Phase 1 (Fin n) | Phase 2 (Finset.range n) |
|---|---|
| `i : Fin n` | `i.val Ôłł Finset.range n` |
| `i.isLt : i.val < n` | `mem_range` converts to `i.val < n` |
| Ordered index access | Unordered set membership |

The bound proof `i.isLt` is the bridge: it's the proof that `Fin n`
carries internally, and it's exactly what `Finset.mem_range` requires.

When you encounter `Finset.range n` in later worlds ÔÇö especially in
summation (`ÔłĹ i Ôłł Finset.range n, f i`) ÔÇö remember that its elements
are exactly the values that `Fin n` indices can take.
"

DisabledTactic trivial ┬źdecide┬╗ native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto

import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L01_FunctionsAsTuples
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L02_VecNotation
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L03_BuildingTuples
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L04_PrependingWithCons
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L05_ConsAccessLemmas
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L06_DroppingTheHead
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L07_HeadTailReconstruction
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L08_RoundTripProperty
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L09_AppendingWithSnoc
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L10_DroppingTheLast
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L11_InitSnocReconstruction
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L12_ExtForFunctions
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L13_TupleComposition
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L14_ConsInjectivity
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L15_SnocInjectivity
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L16_TheEmptyTuple
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L17_ProvingTupleEquality
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L18_FlippingEquations
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L19_ReconstructionStrategy
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L20_Boss

World "FinTuples"
Title "Fin Tuples"

Introduction "
# Fin Tuples: Functions as Ordered Data

You've learned that `Fin n` is the finite index set
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$, and you can navigate within it using
`last`, `castSucc`, and `succ`. Now comes the payoff:

**A function `Fin n Ôćĺ ╬▒` is an `n`-tuple.**

This is one of the most useful identifications in formalized
mathematics. A vector in $\\mathbb{R}^3$ is a function
`Fin 3 Ôćĺ ÔäŁ`. A sequence of length 5 is a function
`Fin 5 Ôćĺ ╬▒`. The entries of an $n \\times m$ matrix are a
function `Fin n Ôćĺ Fin m Ôćĺ ÔäŁ`.

The advantage of using functions instead of lists: the length
is part of the type. You can't accidentally add a 3-vector to
a 4-vector ÔÇö the types don't match.

In this world, you'll learn to:
- **Build** tuples using `![a, b, c]` notation and `Fin.cons`
- **Append** elements using `vecSnoc`
- **Decompose** from both ends: `Fin.tail` (drop first) and `Fin.init` (drop last)
- **Reconstruct**: `Fin.cons_self_tail` (from front) and `vecSnoc_self_init` (from back)
- **Transform** tuples via function composition
- **Prove** two tuples are equal using function extensionality (`ext`)
- **Flip** equations with `.symm` for reconstruction proofs

The central tool is **`ext`**: two tuples are equal if and only
if they agree at every index. You already know `ext` for `Fin`
elements (two `Fin` values are equal when their natural numbers
are equal). Here, `ext` says two functions are equal when they
agree on all inputs ÔÇö same tactic, new context.

The boss at the end will ask you to prove two differently-described
tuples are equal, combining reconstruction from both ends with
extensionality in a single proof.
"

1Ôćĺimport Game.Metadata
     2Ôćĺ
     3ÔćĺWorld "FinTuples"
     4ÔćĺLevel 13
     5Ôćĺ
     6ÔćĺTitle "Composing Tuples with Functions"
     7Ôćĺ
     8ÔćĺIntroduction "
     9Ôćĺ# Transforming Tuples
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺA tuple `f : Fin 3 -> NN` assigns a number to each index.
    12ÔćĺA function `g : NN -> NN` transforms numbers. Their composition
    13Ôćĺ`g circ f : Fin 3 -> NN` transforms the tuple by applying `g`
    14Ôćĺto each entry.
    15Ôćĺ
    16ÔćĺFor example, if `f = ![1, 2, 3]` and `g` doubles its input,
    17Ôćĺthen `g circ f = ![2, 4, 6]` ÔÇö applying `g` to each entry.
    18Ôćĺ
    19ÔćĺThis is the tuple analog of **map** for lists. It foreshadows
    20Ôćĺ`Finset.image` in Phase 2, where you'll apply a function to every
    21Ôćĺelement of a finite set.
    22Ôćĺ
    23Ôćĺ**Strategy**: Use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality, substitute
    24Ôćĺboth formulas, then verify each index by case split.
    25Ôćĺ"
    26Ôćĺ
    27Ôćĺ/-- Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple. -/
    28ÔćĺStatement (f : Fin 3 Ôćĺ ÔäĽ) (g : ÔäĽ Ôćĺ ÔäĽ)
    29Ôćĺ    (hf : f = ![1, 2, 3]) (hg : ÔłÇ x, g x = x * 2) :
    30Ôćĺ    g Ôłś f = ![2, 4, 6] := by
    31Ôćĺ  Hint "Start with `ext ÔčĘv, hvÔčę` to reduce to pointwise equality."
    32Ôćĺ  ext ÔčĘv, hvÔčę
    33Ôćĺ  Hint "The goal has `(g comp f) ÔčĘv, hvÔčę` on the left ÔÇö the
    34Ôćĺ  composition applied to the index. Use `rw [Function.comp_apply]`
    35Ôćĺ  to unfold this to `g (f ÔčĘv, hvÔčę)`, then substitute both
    36Ôćĺ  formulas with `rw [hf, hg]`."
    37Ôćĺ  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]` as
    38Ôćĺ  a single rewrite chain."
    39Ôćĺ  rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]
    40Ôćĺ  Hint "Now case-split on `v` to verify each index."
    41Ôćĺ  cases v with
    42Ôćĺ  | zero => rfl
    43Ôćĺ  | succ n =>
    44Ôćĺ    cases n with
    45Ôćĺ    | zero => rfl
    46Ôćĺ    | succ m =>
    47Ôćĺ      cases m with
    48Ôćĺ      | zero => rfl
    49Ôćĺ      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
    50Ôćĺ
    51ÔćĺConclusion "
    52ÔćĺComposing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple:
    53Ôćĺ`g circ f = ![g (f 0), g (f 1), g (f 2)]`.
    54Ôćĺ
    55ÔćĺThe proof used the same `ext` + case split pattern, but with
    56Ôćĺan extra step: unfolding the composition (`g circ f` means
    57Ôćĺ`fun i => g (f i)`) and substituting both formulas.
    58Ôćĺ
    59Ôćĺ**Why this matters**: In Phase 2, `Finset.image g s` applies a
    60Ôćĺfunction `g` to every element of a finite set `s`. The
    61Ôćĺcomposition `g circ f` is the tuple version of the same idea.
    62ÔćĺUnderstanding tuple transformation prepares you for set-level
    63Ôćĺtransformations.
    64Ôćĺ"
    65Ôćĺ
    66ÔćĺNewTheorem Function.comp_apply
    67Ôćĺ
    68ÔćĺDisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
    69ÔćĺDisabledTheorem funext
    70Ôćĺ

import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 13

Title "Composing Tuples with Functions"

Introduction "
# Transforming Tuples

A tuple `f : Fin 3 -> NN` assigns a number to each index.
A function `g : NN -> NN` transforms numbers. Their composition
`g circ f : Fin 3 -> NN` transforms the tuple by applying `g`
to each entry.

For example, if `f = ![1, 2, 3]` and `g` doubles its input,
then `g circ f = ![2, 4, 6]` ÔÇö applying `g` to each entry.

This is the tuple analog of **map** for lists. It foreshadows
`Finset.image` in Phase 2, where you'll apply a function to every
element of a finite set.

**Strategy**: Use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality, substitute
both formulas, then verify each index by case split.
"

/-- Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 Ôćĺ ÔäĽ) (g : ÔäĽ Ôćĺ ÔäĽ)
    (hf : f = ![1, 2, 3]) (hg : ÔłÇ x, g x = x * 2) :
    g Ôłś f = ![2, 4, 6] := by
  Hint "Start with `ext ÔčĘv, hvÔčę` to reduce to pointwise equality."
  ext ÔčĘv, hvÔčę
  Hint "The goal has `(g comp f) ÔčĘv, hvÔčę` on the left ÔÇö the
  composition applied to the index. Use `rw [Function.comp_apply]`
  to unfold this to `g (f ÔčĘv, hvÔčę)`, then substitute both
  formulas with `rw [hf, hg]`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]` as
  a single rewrite chain."
  rw [Function.comp_apply, hf, hg]
  Hint "Now case-split on `v` to verify each index."
  cases v with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
Composing a tuple with a function gives a new tuple:
`g circ f = ![g (f 0), g (f 1), g (f 2)]`.

The proof used the same `ext` + case split pattern, but with
an extra step: unfolding the composition (`g circ f` means
`fun i => g (f i)`) and substituting both formulas.

**Why this matters**: In Phase 2, `Finset.image g s` applies a
function `g` to every element of a finite set `s`. The
composition `g circ f` is the tuple version of the same idea.
Understanding tuple transformation prepares you for set-level
transformations.
"

NewTheorem Function.comp_apply

DisabledTheorem funext

import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 14

Title "Cons Injectivity"

Introduction "
# Fin.cons Determines Its Inputs

You've learned how to *read* a cons'd tuple using the access
lemmas: `Fin.cons_val_zero` and `Fin.cons_val_succ`. Now let's
prove the converse: **if two cons'd tuples are equal, their
heads and tails must be equal**.

This is the *injectivity* of `Fin.cons`: no information is lost
when you prepend. If `Fin.cons a f = Fin.cons b g`, then `a = b`
and `f = g`.

**New tool: `congrFun`**. If `h : f = g`, then `congrFun h a`
gives you `f a = g a` ÔÇö the functions agree at input `a`. This
is the **pointwise extraction** move.

**Strategy**:
For the head: `have h0 := congrFun h 0` extracts agreement at
index `0`, then simplify with `Fin.cons_val_zero`.
For the tail: `ext i`, then `have hi := congrFun h i.succ`
extracts agreement at `i.succ`, then simplify with
`Fin.cons_val_succ`.

**Your task**: Prove that `Fin.cons` is injective.
"

/-- If two cons'd tuples are equal, their heads and tails match. -/
Statement (a b : ÔäĽ) (f g : Fin 2 Ôćĺ ÔäĽ)
    (h : (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 Ôćĺ ÔäĽ) = Fin.cons b g) :
    a = b Ôłž f = g := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split into two goals: heads equal
  and tails equal."
  constructor
  ┬Ě -- heads are equal
    Hint "Extract what `h` says at index `0`:
    `have h0 := congrFun h 0`.
    This gives `h0 : (Fin.cons a f) 0 = (Fin.cons b g) 0`."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`have h0 := congrFun h 0`"
    have h0 := congrFun h 0
    Hint "Now simplify both sides using the access lemma:
    `rw [Fin.cons_val_zero, Fin.cons_val_zero] at h0`"
    rw [Fin.cons_val_zero, Fin.cons_val_zero] at h0
    Hint "Now `{h0}` is exactly the goal. Use `exact h0`."
    exact h0
  ┬Ě -- tails are equal
    Hint "Use `ext i` to reduce function equality to pointwise
    equality."
    ext i
    Hint "Extract agreement at index `i.succ`:
    `have hi := congrFun h i.succ`."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`have hi := congrFun h i.succ`"
    have hi := congrFun h i.succ
    Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Fin.cons_val_succ, Fin.cons_val_succ] at hi`
    then `exact hi`"
    rw [Fin.cons_val_succ, Fin.cons_val_succ] at hi
    exact hi

NewTheorem congrFun

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons` is **injective**: equal outputs mean equal inputs.
The proof pattern uses **pointwise extraction** via `congrFun`:

1. `have h0 := congrFun h 0` ÔÇö extract agreement at a specific index
2. Simplify with the access lemma to relate inputs
3. For the tail, use `ext` to reduce to pointwise equality first

`congrFun` is the clean way to go from a function equality
`h : f = g` to a value equality `f a = g a` at a specific input.

The same idea works for `vecSnoc`: if `vecSnoc f x = vecSnoc g y`,
then `x = y` (extract at `Fin.last`) and `f = g`
(extract at `i.castSucc`). You'll prove this in the next level.

Note: the boss level tests different abilities (reconstruction
strategies and index-by-index verification), not injectivity proofs.
"

    10Ôćĺ

    12Ôćĺequal by showing their underlying values are equal. Now you'll
    13Ôćĺuse `ext` in a new context: **functions**.
    14Ôćĺ

    16Ôćĺ
    17Ôćĺ> Two functions are equal if and only if they agree on every input.
    18Ôćĺ

    22Ôćĺ`ext i` introduces `i : Fin n` and changes the goal to `f i = g i`.

    26ÔćĺHere, `ext` decomposes function equality into pointwise equality.
    27Ôćĺ

    34Ôćĺ  Hint "Use `ext i` to introduce an index `i : Fin 3` and

    39Ôćĺ  exact h i

    41ÔćĺConclusion "

    43Ôćĺ
    44Ôćĺ1. `ext i` ÔÇö reduce function equality to pointwise equality
    45Ôćĺ2. Prove `f i = g i` for the introduced `i`
    46Ôćĺ
    47ÔćĺThis is the same tactic you used in MeetFin for `Fin` elements.
    48ÔćĺBoth are instances of the same principle: two composite objects
    49Ôćĺare equal when their components are equal.
    50Ôćĺ
    51ÔćĺNote: the tactic `funext i` does the same thing as `ext i`
    52Ôćĺfor functions. Both are valid.
    53Ôćĺ"
    54Ôćĺ
    55Ôćĺ/-- `ext` reduces equality of composite objects to equality of their components.
    56Ôćĺ
    57Ôćĺ## For Fin elements
    58ÔćĺWhen the goal is `a = b` where `a b : Fin n`, `ext` reduces it to
    59Ôćĺ`a.val = b.val` ÔÇö two `Fin` values are equal when their underlying
    60Ôćĺnatural numbers are equal.
    61Ôćĺ
    62Ôćĺ## For functions
    63ÔćĺWhen the goal is `f = g` where `f g : ╬▒ Ôćĺ ╬▓`, `ext i` introduces
    64Ôćĺa variable `i : ╬▒` and reduces the goal to `f i = g i` ÔÇö two
    65Ôćĺfunctions are equal when they agree on every input.
    66Ôćĺ
    67Ôćĺ## For finsets
    68ÔćĺWhen the goal is `s = t` where `s t : Finset ╬▒`, `ext x` introduces
    69Ôćĺa variable `x : ╬▒` and reduces the goal to `x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t` ÔÇö two
    70Ôćĺfinsets are equal when they have the same elements (extensionality).
    71Ôćĺ
    72ÔćĺAll are instances of the same principle: two composite objects are
    73Ôćĺequal when their components are equal.

import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 12

Title "ext for Functions"

Introduction "
# Extensionality for Functions

In MeetFin, you used `ext` to prove two `Fin` elements are
equal by showing their underlying values are equal. Now you'll
use `ext` in a new context: **functions**.

For functions, extensionality says:

> Two functions are equal if and only if they agree on every input.

In symbols: $f = g \\iff \\forall\\, i,\\; f(i) = g(i)$.

When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : Fin n Ôćĺ ╬▒`, using
`ext i` introduces `i : Fin n` and changes the goal to `f i = g i`.

This is the same `ext` tactic, just applied to a different type.
In MeetFin, `ext` decomposed `Fin` equality into value equality.
Here, `ext` decomposes function equality into pointwise equality.

**Your task**: Prove `f = g` given that they agree on every input.
"

/-- Two functions that agree everywhere are equal. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 3 Ôćĺ ÔäĽ) (h : ÔłÇ i, f i = g i) :
    f = g := by
  Hint "Use `ext i` to introduce an index `i : Fin 3` and
  reduce the goal to `f i = g i`."
  ext i
  Hint "The goal is `f i = g i`. The hypothesis `{h}` says
  `ÔłÇ i, f i = g i`. Use `exact h i`."
  exact h i

Conclusion "
The pattern is simple:

1. `ext i` ÔÇö reduce function equality to pointwise equality
2. Prove `f i = g i` for the introduced `i`

This is the same tactic you used in MeetFin for `Fin` elements.
Both are instances of the same principle: two composite objects
are equal when their components are equal.

Note: the tactic `funext i` does the same thing as `ext i`
for functions. Both are valid.
"

/-- `ext` reduces equality of composite objects to equality of their components.

## For Fin elements
When the goal is `a = b` where `a b : Fin n`, `ext` reduces it to
`a.val = b.val` ÔÇö two `Fin` values are equal when their underlying
natural numbers are equal.

## For functions
When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : ╬▒ Ôćĺ ╬▓`, `ext i` introduces
a variable `i : ╬▒` and reduces the goal to `f i = g i` ÔÇö two
functions are equal when they agree on every input.

## For finsets
When the goal is `s = t` where `s t : Finset ╬▒`, `ext x` introduces
a variable `x : ╬▒` and reduces the goal to `x Ôłł s Ôćö x Ôłł t` ÔÇö two
finsets are equal when they have the same elements (extensionality).

All are instances of the same principle: two composite objects are
equal when their components are equal.
-/
TacticDoc ext

import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L01_WhatIsAFinset
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L02_FindingDeeperElements
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L03_TheEmptyFinset
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L04_RangeOfNaturals
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L05_TheRangeBoundary
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L06_RangeEmpty
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L07_VariableRange
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L08_RangeReverseDirection
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L09_FinToFinsetBridge
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L10_GeneralNonMembership
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L11_ProvingNonMembership
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L12_ReadingMembership
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L13_InsertIsIdempotent
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L14_NonemptyFinsets
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L15_ProvingNonempty
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L16_DecideShortcut
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L17_Boss

World "FinsetBasics"
Title "Finset Basics"

Introduction "
# Finset Basics: Finite Sets

In Phase 1, you learned about `Fin n` ÔÇö a finite *index type* where
each element is a number with a bound proof. Functions `Fin n Ôćĺ ╬▒`
gave you *ordered tuples*: data accessed by position.

But not everything in mathematics has a natural ordering. The set of
prime divisors of 30 is $\\{2, 3, 5\\}$ ÔÇö there's no reason 2 should
come 'first'. The elements of a group satisfying some property form a
*collection defined by membership*, not by position.

This is where `Finset` comes in. A `Finset ╬▒` is an **unordered finite
collection** of elements from `╬▒`, with no duplicates and decidable
membership. Think of it as a mathematical finite set, formalized.

The key operations:
- **Literal notation**: `\\{1, 2, 3\\}` builds a concrete finset
- **`Ôłů`**: the empty finset
- **`insert a s`**: add an element (no-op if already present)
- **`Finset.range n`**: the set $\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$

The key proof move: to show `x Ôłł s`, unfold the set structure using
*membership lemmas* (`Finset.mem_insert`, `Finset.mem_singleton`,
`Finset.mem_range`) until the goal reduces to something `rfl` or
`omega` can close.

The bridge from Phase 1: `Finset.range n` contains exactly the same
elements as `Fin n` ÔÇö the numbers $0$ through $n-1$. Where `Fin n`
gave you *indexed access* (`f i` for `i : Fin n`), `Finset.range n`
gives you *membership* (`k Ôłł Finset.range n Ôćö k < n`). Same elements,
different interface.

In this world you'll learn to prove membership, non-membership,
and basic structural facts about finsets. The boss at the end
combines all these moves.
"

import GameServer
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.Basic
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation
import Mathlib.Data.Finset.Basic
import Mathlib.Data.Finset.Card

/-! ## Documentation for disabled tactics and theorems

These docs are needed by the GameServer when tactics/theorems are disabled.
They are placed here so all level files can reference them.
-/

/-! ### NNG4-baseline tactics

These tactics are assumed known from the Natural Number Game or
equivalent. They are introduced in Level 1 so the game doesn't warn
about missing introductions.
-/

/-- `exact e` closes the goal if `e` has exactly the type of the goal.

## Syntax
```
exact expression
```

## When to use it
When you have a term (variable, function application, constructor)
whose type matches the goal exactly.
-/
TacticDoc exact

/-- `rw [h]` rewrites the goal using an equation or iff `h`.

## Syntax
```
rw [h]           -- rewrite the goal
rw [h] at hyp    -- rewrite a hypothesis
rw [ÔćÉ h]         -- rewrite right-to-left
rw [h1, h2, h3]  -- chain multiple rewrites
```

## When to use it
When you have an equation `h : a = b` or iff `h : P Ôćö Q` and want
to replace `a` with `b` (or `P` with `Q`) in the goal or a hypothesis.
-/
TacticDoc rw

/-- `intro x` introduces a universally quantified variable or an
antecedent of an implication into the context.

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetBasics"
Level 10

Title "The General Boundary"

Introduction "
# n is Never in Finset.range n

In Level 5, you proved `5 Ôłë Finset.range 5` ÔÇö the boundary element
is excluded from the range. That was a concrete instance.

Now prove the **general** version: for any `n`, `n` itself is not
in `Finset.range n`. This parallels MeetFin Level 12, where you
moved from `Fin 5` to `Fin (n + 1)` ÔÇö the same skills work with
variables.

**Strategy**: The same non-membership pattern from Level 5, now
with a variable `n` instead of the concrete `5`.
"

/-- n is never a member of Finset.range n. -/
Statement (n : ÔäĽ) : n Ôłë Finset.range n := by
  Hint "Assume membership: `intro h`."
  intro h
  Hint "Rewrite to extract the inequality:
  `rw [Finset.mem_range] at h`."
  rw [Finset.mem_range] at h
  Hint "Now `{h}` says `n < n`, which is impossible. `omega`

import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFinset"
Level 4

Title "Union and Intersection Subset"

Introduction "
# Union Retrieval

Prove that $(s \\cap t) \\cup u \\subseteq s \\cup u$.

The intersection $s \\cap t$ is contained in $s$, so anything in
$(s \\cap t) \\cup u$ is either in $s$ (via the intersection) or
in $u$ ÔÇö both land in $s \\cup u$.

This combines `subset_iff`, `mem_union`, `mem_inter`, and `cases`.
"

/-- Intersecting before taking a union can only shrink the first component. -/
Statement (s t u : Finset ÔäĽ) : s Ôłę t Ôł¬ u ÔŐć s Ôł¬ u := by
  Hint "A subset proof means: for every element on the left, show
  it belongs to the right. How do you unfold a subset goal?"
  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
  intro x hx
  Hint (hidden := true) "Unfold `hx` with `rw [Finset.mem_union] at hx`,
  then `rw [Finset.mem_union]` on the goal and case-split with
  `cases hx with`. In the `inl` case, unfold the intersection to
  extract `x Ôłł s`. In the `inr` case, `x Ôłł u` maps directly."
  rw [Finset.mem_union] at hx
  rw [Finset.mem_union]
  cases hx with
  | inl h =>
    rw [Finset.mem_inter] at h
    left
    exact h.1
  | inr h =>
    right
    exact h

Conclusion "
The case split on union membership is the key move. Each branch
routes to the correct side of the target union:
- From $s \\cap t$: extract $x \\in s$, then `left`
- From $u$: directly `right`
"

/-- `sup_le_sup_right` states monotonicity of ÔŐö on the right:
if `a ÔëĄ b` then `a ÔŐö c ÔëĄ b ÔŐö c`. -/
TheoremDoc sup_le_sup_right as "sup_le_sup_right" in "Order"

/-- `sup_le_sup_left` states monotonicity of ÔŐö on the left:
if `a ÔëĄ b` then `c ÔŐö a ÔëĄ c ÔŐö b`. -/
TheoremDoc sup_le_sup_left as "sup_le_sup_left" in "Order"

/-- `Finset.union_subset_iff` states `s Ôł¬ t ÔŐć u Ôćö s ÔŐć u Ôłž t ÔŐć u`. -/
TheoremDoc Finset.union_subset_iff as "Finset.union_subset_iff" in "Finset"

import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFinset"
Level 5

Title "Union Complement"

Introduction "
# Subtracting a Component from a Union

Prove that $(s \\cup t) \\setminus t \\subseteq s$.

If $x$ is in $s \\cup t$ but not in $t$, then $x$ must have come
from $s$.

The proof combines `mem_sdiff`, `mem_union`, and `cases`.
"

/-- Removing one component from a union leaves a subset of the other. -/
Statement (s t : Finset ÔäĽ) : (s Ôł¬ t) \ t ÔŐć s := by
  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
  intro x hx
  Hint "Unfold `hx` with `rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at hx` then
  `obtain` the parts."
  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at hx
  obtain ÔčĘhut, hntÔčę := hx
  rw [Finset.mem_union] at hut
  Hint "You have `hut : x Ôłł s ÔłĘ x Ôłł t` and `hnt : x Ôłë t`.
  Case-split on the disjunction ÔÇö one branch gives the goal directly,
  the other contradicts `hnt`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Use `cases hut with`. In `inl`, you have
  `hxs : x Ôłł s` ÔÇö that's the goal. In `inr`, you have `hxt : x Ôłł t`
  contradicting `hnt` ÔÇö use `exact absurd hxt hnt`."
  cases hut with
  | inl hxs => exact hxs
  | inr hxt => exact absurd hxt hnt

Conclusion "
A direct case split resolved this cleanly:
- **inl** ($x \\in s$): this IS the goal ÔÇö `exact hxs`
- **inr** ($x \\in t$): contradicts `hnt : x \\notin t` ÔÇö
  `absurd hxt hnt` closes the goal

No `by_contra` needed. When a disjunction has one branch that
gives the goal directly and another that contradicts a hypothesis,
a direct case split is the simplest approach.
"

/-- `sdiff_le_self` states `a \ b ÔëĄ a` in a lattice. -/
TheoremDoc sdiff_le_self as "sdiff_le_self" in "Order"

/-- `Finset.sdiff_subset` states `s \ t ÔŐć s`. -/
TheoremDoc Finset.sdiff_subset as "Finset.sdiff_subset" in "Finset"

/-- `sdiff_le` states `a \ b ÔëĄ a` (alternate name for sdiff_le_self). -/
TheoremDoc sdiff_le as "sdiff_le" in "Order"

/-- `sdiff_le_iff` states `a \ b ÔëĄ c Ôćö a ÔëĄ b ÔŐö c` in a Heyting algebra. -/
TheoremDoc sdiff_le_iff as "sdiff_le_iff" in "Order"

/-- `sdiff_le_iff'` is the commuted form: `a \ b ÔëĄ c Ôćö a ÔëĄ c ÔŐö b`. -/
TheoremDoc sdiff_le_iff' as "sdiff_le_iff'" in "Order"

/-- `sdiff_le_iff_left` states `a \ b ÔëĄ b Ôćö a ÔëĄ b`. -/
TheoremDoc sdiff_le_iff_left as "sdiff_le_iff_left" in "Order"

World "PsetFinset"
Title "Problem Set: Finsets"

Introduction "
# Problem Set: Finsets

You've completed three lecture worlds on finsets:
- **FinsetBasics**: membership, range, insert, singleton, nonempty
- **FinsetOperations**: union, intersection, sdiff, filter, subset, ext, by_contra
- **FinsetImage**: image, forward/backward membership, cardinality, injectivity

Now apply these skills to **fresh problems** with sparser hints and
no new definitions. One new bridge fact ÔÇö `Finset.card_range` ÔÇö
connects range to cardinality.

**Note**: `simp` is disabled throughout this pset, and this also
blocks `simp only`. All proofs use manual `rw` chains ÔÇö this is
intentional, testing your fundamental rewriting skills.
"
